bar.name: Escala
bar.happyHour: Sun-Wed 11:30am-12am, Thu-Sat 11:30am-2am — Colombian/Korean fusion with DJ brunch Sundays
bar.vibe: Colombian-Korean fusion mashup inside Chapman Plaza, bright colorful interior, DJ culture, urban art-inspired, full bar and late-night
barFacts: Located inside Chapman Plaza — the 1929 Spanish Revival complex that was America's first drive-in grocery store
Opened 2014 as a mashup of Colombian and Korean flavors, with initial menu by Chef Chris Oh and later additions by Colombia's Chef Alex DJ Skull Torres
Sits in a courtyard complex originally designed by Morgan, Walls & Clements — the same firm that built the Wiltern Theatre and Pellissier Building
The 'Escala' name means 'scale' or 'stop' in Spanish — fitting for a bar inside a building designed for automobiles to drive under a high arch and park in the central courtyard

stops.label: Chapman Plaza / America's First Drive-In Market
Ambassador Hotel Site / RFK Community Schools

pois.name: Chapman Plaza (Chapman Park Market)
facts: America's first drive-in grocery store — automobiles drove under a high arch and parked in the central courtyard
Designed by Morgan, Walls & Clements in Spanish Revival style with Churrigueresque towers — same firm that built the Wiltern Theatre and Pellissier Building
Built to serve the wealthy families of Fremont Place, Windsor Square, and Hancock Park who drove to buy groceries
50,000 square feet of fortress-like facade with thick concrete walls and ornate towers
Interior courtyard featured groceries, shopfronts, lush landscaping, statues and fountains
Restored in 1990 by Wayne Ratkovich and architect Brenda Levin — the same pair who saved the Wiltern from demolition in 1981
Now houses Escala, KazuNori, Uncle Tetsu, Origin Korean BBQ, and other Koreatown dining in its arcade
The Chapman family also donated the land for the first church in Wilshire Center (now Oasis Church) across the intersection

features.detail: Spanish Revival facade with Churrigueresque towers and ornate detailing
Large entry arch — originally for automobiles to drive through into courtyard
Central courtyard with arched windows and double-wide hallways
Fountains and statues in courtyard
Red-tiled roof typical of Spanish Revival

connections.link: Same firm designed both — Stiles O. Clements was lead architect on all three
Chapman family donated the land for the first church in Wilshire Center, directly across the intersection
Same developer-architect team restored both Chapman Plaza (1990) and the Wiltern (1985)
Both built during Wilshire Center's 1920s boom, five blocks apart on the same corridor

pois.name: The Gaylord Apartments & HMS Bounty
facts: 13-story Early Renaissance Revival apartment tower designed by Walker & Eisen — the same firm that designed the Beverly Wilshire Hotel and Hotel Normandie
Named for Henry Gaylord Wilshire, socialist land developer and founder of Wilshire Boulevard — LA's most famous socialist namesake
Marketed as 'own-your-own' apartments with LA's first central refrigeration system letting tenants freeze their own ice cubes
The LA Times called it 'one of the largest and most pretentious apartment houses in the country' when it opened
Ground-floor bar was originally called 'The Gay Room' (named for Gaylord Wilshire), then 'The Secret Harbor,' before becoming HMS Bounty in 1962
HMS Bounty was frequented by Jack Webb, Walter Winchell, and William Randolph Hearst when previous establishments occupied the space
Built directly across Wilshire from the Ambassador Hotel — the two buildings anchored the neighborhood's glamour era
Featured in Mad Men and other film/TV productions for its preserved mid-century nautical interior
199 units, 50.3m tall — one of the tallest 1920s residential buildings on Wilshire

features.detail: 13-story Early Renaissance Revival tower — brick and terra cotta
Nautical-themed bar interior with wood paneling, red leather booths
Ornamental cornice and window detailing at upper stories

connections.link: Same firm designed both — Percy Eisen and Albert Walker were among LA's most prolific 1920s architects
Built directly across Wilshire from each other — the Gaylord's residents could look down at the Ambassador's Cocoanut Grove
Socialist who gave his name to LA's most famous boulevard — and this building
Both luxury apartment towers opened the same year on Wilshire, six blocks apart

pois.name: Ambassador Hotel Site / RFK Community Schools
facts: The Ambassador Hotel (1921-2006) was a 1,200-room entertainment complex with 37 shops, a golf course, bowling alley, and the legendary 1,000-seat Cocoanut Grove nightclub
Designed by architect Myron Hunt; later renovated by Paul Williams, first Black AIA fellow
Hosted 6 Academy Awards ceremonies between 1930 and 1943, and every US president from Hoover to Nixon
The Cocoanut Grove featured papier-mache palm trees recycled from Rudolph Valentino's film 'The Sheik,' with mechanical monkeys hanging from them
Senator Robert F. Kennedy was shot in the hotel's kitchen pantry on June 5, 1968 after winning the California Democratic presidential primary
The hotel served alcohol during Prohibition — partly why it became Hollywood's gathering place
Closed 1989, demolished 2005-2006 after a decade-long preservation battle led by the LA Conservancy

Replaced by RFK Community Schools ($578 million — most expensive public school in US history), which preserved the Cocoanut Grove as a 582-seat theatre and reconstructed the Embassy Ballroom vaulted ceiling as a student library

The Ava Gardner mural on the school exterior (by Beau Stanton, 2016) sparked a 3-year controversy — its sun-ray motif was seen as resembling the Imperial Japanese battle flag. Revised in 2021 to include Korean cultural imagery

features.detail: Modern school complex echoing the Ambassador's massing — sloped roof, large lawn, contemporary palette
Revised Ava Gardner / Koreatown mural on school exterior wall (2021 version)
Preserved Cocoanut Grove now functioning as 582-seat school theatre
Marble memorial to RFK
Public park (RFK Inspiration Park) on the grounds

connections.link: Built directly across Wilshire from each other — the Gaylord's residents witnessed the Ambassador's entire arc from glamour to decline
The Haig was in a bungalow directly across the street from the Ambassador — West Coast jazz met Hollywood supper club culture
The assassination and hotel's subsequent decline paralleled the neighborhood's transformation from old-money LA to immigrant gateway
The mural debate encapsulates the neighborhood's layered history — Hollywood glamour, Japanese colonial trauma, Korean-American community

pois.name: Hotel Normandie
pois.address: 605 S Normandie Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90005
facts: 1926 Renaissance Revival hotel designed by Walker & Eisen — same firm that designed the Gaylord and the Beverly Wilshire
Author Malcolm Lowry wrote significant portions of 'Under the Volcano' while living here — now ranked #11 on Modern Library's 100 Best Novels
Operated continuously for 90 years, though by 2011 it was barely functioning with rooms littered with trash and some housing illegal activities
Acquired in 2010 for $4.4M by preservationist Jingbo Lou, who spent $5M restoring it into an upscale boutique hotel
Now houses Cassell's Hamburgers (founded 1948) on the ground floor — one of LA's original burger institutions
92 guest rooms and suites in the restored building

features.detail: Renaissance Revival facade — 1926 detailing preserved through restoration
21.3m height (from Overpass data)
Cassell's Hamburgers signage at ground floor

connections.link: Same firm designed both within two years
Wrote parts of Under the Volcano here — a novel about an alcoholic British consul in Mexico, written in a fading LA hotel
The hotel's arc from luxury to squalor to boutique revival mirrors the entire neighborhood

pois.name: Immanuel Presbyterian Church
facts: French Gothic Revival church with a 205-foot stone-clad corner tower — one of the tallest church towers in LA
Designed by architect Chauncey Fitch Skilling, completed 1929
Steel-framed tower allows for a strikingly spacious interior despite the Gothic appearance
Stained glass rose window above the entry, with windows throughout by Dixon Art Glass Company of Los Angeles
Interior features beam trusses, columns, arches, carved wood pulpit, chandeliers and oak furnishings
Congregation established 1888 as a spinoff from First Presbyterian Church in downtown LA — has occupied 3 buildings
The rose window at Oasis Church across Wilshire is said to be a copy of one in Rheims Cathedral — two monumental churches face each other across the boulevard

features.detail: 205-foot French Gothic tower — stone-clad with steel frame
Stained glass rose window above main entry
62m height (from Overpass data)
Gothic arches, beam trusses, carved wood pulpit visible through doors

connections.link: Both completed in 1929 — the neighborhood's last pre-Depression building boom
Two monumental churches face each other across Wilshire — both with rose windows, both LA landmarks

pois.name: Oasis Church (Wilshire Christian Church)
pois.address: 634 S Normandie Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90005
facts: First church built in Wilshire Center — Chapman family donated the land
Massive Romanesque structure with Byzantine elements, designed by Robert H. Orr for maximum visibility from passing cars
200-foot tower on a corner orientation — designed to be seen from Wilshire Boulevard at driving speed
Rose window by Judson Studios, said to be a copy of one in Rheims Cathedral in France
The original 1911 church on this site was a bungalow-style structure — replaced by this building in 1927 as the neighborhood became a major boulevard
Built 1927 — the same era as the architect-designed-for-cars philosophy that shaped the entire Wilshire corridor

features.detail: 200-foot Romanesque/Byzantine tower on corner
Rose window by Judson Studios
47.3m height (from Overpass data)
Massive stone facade with Romanesque arches

connections.link: Same family that built Chapman Plaza donated the land for this church
Two monumental churches face each other across Wilshire
Both designed for the automobile — the church tower to be seen at speed, the market to be driven into

pois.name: The Talmadge
facts: 12-story brick apartment tower named for silent film actress Norma Talmadge, owned by her husband — movie studio executive Joseph M. Schenck
Designed by William and Alexander Curlett & Claud Beelman — Beelman later designed the Eastern Columbia Building
Rose on the site of a Craftsman mansion designed by Greene & Greene for automobile dealer Earle C. Anthony in 1909 — the house was moved to Beverly Hills to make way
Opened with a summer garden party attended by socialites and business leaders; the Schenks lived on the tenth floor
49 units — originally marketed to socialites and heirs
One of the first luxury high-rises on Wilshire Boulevard, anchoring the corridor's shift from residential mansions to apartment living

features.detail: 12-story brick residential tower
47.3m height (from Overpass data)
Renaissance Revival detailing

connections.link: A silent film power couple named an apartment building — the era's equivalent of a vanity project
Both luxury apartment towers opened the same year on Wilshire
A masterpiece Craftsman house was literally moved to Beverly Hills to make room for this tower — the neighborhood's identity shift in one transaction

pois.name: The Haig (demolished)
pois.address: 638 S Kenmore Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90005
facts: Premier West Coast jazz club of the 1950s — 'the best-known Los Angeles jazz club of the day' (James Lincoln Collier)
Originally a bungalow home converted by owner John Bennett into a club — 'looked more like a doll house than a club'
Located directly across from the Ambassador Hotel and its Cocoanut Grove supper club
In early 1952, Gerry Mulligan walked in, found Erroll Garner and Bobby Short jamming without amplification, and joined in — leading to his legendary pianoless quartet with Chet Baker
Hampton Hawes, Warne Marsh, Lee Konitz, Bud Shank, Red Norvo's trio, Wardell Gray, and Curtis Counce all played here
Closed April 4, 1956; demolished one year later for a parking garage
The club where West Coast cool jazz was essentially born — destroyed for parking

features.detail: Nothing remains — the site is now a parking structure. The jazz is gone.

connections.link: Directly across the street — a bungalow jazz club facing a 1,000-seat Hollywood supper club
Both landmarks obliterated — the neighborhood's cultural history exists only in memory

pois.name: Equitable Life Building
facts: 138m (453 ft) tall, 34 floors — tied with LA City Hall for 43rd-tallest building in Los Angeles
International Style skyscraper designed by Welton Becket & Associates for Equitable Life Insurance Company
Facade of precast concrete sandblasted to expose beige Texas limestone aggregate
Houses the Philippine Consulate General on the 5th floor since 2015
Also houses the Consulate General of Indonesia next door at 3457 Wilshire (built 1946) — two Southeast Asian consulates bookend the block
Lobby contains 'Equitable Vitrines' — glass cases hosting rotating contemporary art exhibitions
Consulate of the Republic of Korea is also nearby at 3243 Wilshire — three Asian-Pacific consulates within 3 blocks

features.detail: 34-story International Style tower — tallest building in Koreatown
features.material: precast concrete with exposed Texas limestone aggregate

connections.link: Three Asian-Pacific consulates within 3 blocks — the neighborhood is a de facto diplomatic quarter

pois.name: Embassy Apartments
pois.address: 706 S Mariposa Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90005
facts: 1926 apartment building — 34 units, 19.1m tall
Part of the dense 1920s apartment stock surrounding the Ambassador Hotel
Representative of the Mid-Wilshire apartment construction boom

features.detail: 1920s apartment building facade

pois.name: Ava Gardner / Koreatown Mural (revised 2021)
facts: Original mural by Beau Stanton (2016) depicted Ava Gardner's profile with sun-ray motif as tribute to the Ambassador Hotel's Cocoanut Grove
Korean Americans objected: the radiating lines resembled the Imperial Japanese battle flag used during Japan's colonial occupation of Korea (1910-1945)
3-year community debate — even Shepard Fairey defended the original artist
Revised 2021: now includes a traditional Korean phoenix pattern, a migrant worker harvesting oranges from a historic photograph, and a 1935 Koreatown hotel worker in uniform
The mural is a physical artifact of the neighborhood's identity collision — Hollywood nostalgia, Korean-American trauma, immigrant community assertion

features.detail: Large exterior mural on school wall — Ava Gardner profile with Korean cultural imagery (revised version)
Korean phoenix pattern and immigrant worker figures integrated into design

connections.link: The original was a tribute to the Cocoanut Grove nightclub that once stood on this exact site
A mural honoring Hollywood's past collided with the Korean community's colonial trauma — on the site of RFK's assassination. History layered on history.

pois.name: 6th Street Streetcar Commercial Historic District
pois.address: 6th St at Oxford Ave and 6th St at Western Ave
designations: Eligible for National Register of Historic Places
facts: Eligible for the National Register as a rare intact example of 1920s streetcar commercialization in Los Angeles
Developed around the streetcar track that ran down 6th Street — all buildings were built while the streetcar was operating
Architectural styles: Italian Renaissance, Tudor Revival, Art Deco — all with pedestrian-oriented storefronts and no automobile accommodation on parcels
Most of LA's streetcar corridors have been demolished or made unrecognizable — this concentration of intact buildings is extremely rare
Six contributing buildings, all with storefronts on the sidewalk
The district is the commercial counterpart to the Wilshire corridor's car-oriented design — 6th Street was for streetcar riders, Wilshire was for drivers

features.detail: Intact 1920s commercial storefronts — Italian Renaissance, Tudor Revival, Art Deco styles
Buildings set directly on the sidewalk with no setback — designed for streetcar pedestrians, not cars
Consistent 1920s parcel patterns and building massing

connections.link: Two parallel corridors, two transportation philosophies — 6th Street for trolley riders, Wilshire for drivers. Chapman Plaza bridged both.
Chapman Plaza was built at the intersection of the streetcar corridor and the automobile corridor

pois.name: Cafe Jack (Titanic Cafe)
pois.address: 508 S Western Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90020
facts: A cafe built to look like the Titanic, anchored on Western Avenue between an H&R Block and a dry cleaner
Owner Jack Shin saw James Cameron's film hundreds of times and changed his legal name to Jack after DiCaprio's character
Shin built the entire bow — black and white hull, yellow trim, white railings — with his bare hands
Interior is a maze of private rooms, karaoke rooms, patios, and snugs decorated with hearts, twinkling lights, and fake plants
Shin gives tarot card readings in his booth between serving Asian fusion sushi and shaved ice
Atlas Obscura calls it 'a tarot-reading Titanic superfan's cafe shrine'

features.detail: Full-scale Titanic bow structure protruding from storefront

features.text: Cafe Jack signage with nautical elements

features.detail: Captain's wheel, engine order telegraph, and Titanic memorabilia visible through entrance

pois.routeNote: Outside 500m radius — supplemental POI for extended hunt

pois.name: The Line Hotel / Break Room 86
facts: The Line Hotel occupies the former Radisson Wilshire Plaza (1965) — now a Sydell Group property
Break Room 86 is an 80s-themed speakeasy entered through a loading dock off Ardmore Avenue, past hotel kitchens and ice machines
The actual entrance is a vending machine that swivels to reveal the hidden bar
Interior: stacked TVs playing Garfield and Ghostbusters, a wall covered in cassette tapes, VHS cassette menus, vintage arcade games, high school lockers
Originally designed as the actual hotel employee break room — repurposed into a bar
Rooftop features Openaire, a greenhouse restaurant by Josiah Citrin

features.detail: 380-unit hotel tower, 43.8m tall
Loading dock entrance on Ardmore Ave leads to hidden bar
features.location: west side of building, Ardmore Ave

stop1_chapmanPlaza.notes: 'What kind of building has an arch big enough to drive a car through?' is a reframe. Churrigueresque towers are countable (spotter). The Chapman family connection to Oasis Church across the street is a connector. Morgan Walls & Clements linking to the Wiltern is a narrative thread. The courtyard fountains/statues are photo-challenge material. 'America's first ___' is a cipher-lite source.

stop2_gaylordHmsBounty.notes: The name evolution (Gay Room → Secret Harbor → HMS Bounty) is a perfect sequence. 'Named for LA's most famous socialist' is a narrative riddle. Walker & Eisen connecting to Hotel Normandie is a connector. The 13 stories and 199 units are countable. The ice cube refrigeration innovation is a reframe. Henry Gaylord Wilshire's socialism is unexpected material.

stop3_ambassadorSite.notes: The Ambassador's arc (1921 hotel → 6 Oscars → RFK assassination → demolished → $578M school) is a narrative sequence. 'Papier-mache palm trees from a Valentino film' is a narrative riddle. The Ava Gardner mural controversy (Hollywood vs. Korean colonial trauma) is a reframe. The RFK memorial is a spotter. The Cocoanut Grove preserved as a school theatre is a connector between eras. Most expensive public school in US history is a cipher-lite source.

overpassBuildingStock.preWar: ~130 (estimated from decade breakdown: 1920s-1930s account for majority)
overpassBuildingStock.note: Koreatown's building stock is heavily weighted toward the 1920s-1960s. The 1920s boom produced most of the architectural landmarks (Gaylord, Talmadge, Hotel Normandie, Chapman Plaza, churches). A second wave of mid-century construction (1950s-1960s) added commercial towers. The neighborhood retains its 1920s bones more than most LA districts.

metadata.coverageNotes: Koreatown/Mid-Wilshire has excellent coverage. Wikipedia returned 19 geosearch hits (strong). The Ambassador Hotel site, Chapman Plaza, and the cluster of 1920s buildings provide deep historical material. The neighborhood's identity layers — old-money LA, Hollywood glamour, Korean-American community, immigrant gateway — create rich connective tissue between POIs. The demolished sites (Ambassador, The Haig) are ghosts with strong stories but no physical observable, balanced by the surviving 1920s buildings.

metadata.keyConnection: Escala sits inside Chapman Plaza, which was designed by the same firm (Morgan, Walls & Clements) that built the Wiltern Theatre. The same developer-architect pair (Ratkovich/Levin) restored both buildings. The Chapman family that built the market also donated the land for Oasis Church across the street. This is the densest web of connections in the profile — the bar's own building is the best clue source.
